content.summary: Build a GPU-accelerated particle system using GLSL TOPs to simulate and render thousands of particles in real-time. This tutorial covers encoding particle state in textures, writing simulation logic in GLSL, and rendering particles using instancing with a Geometry COMP.

sections.title: Particle State as Textures
sections.content:
In GPU particle systems, each pixel in a texture represents one particle. We use two textures: one for **position** (XYZ stored in RGB) and one for **velocity** (XYZ in RGB). A 256x256 texture gives us 65,536 particles.

Create two **Noise TOPs** at 256x256 resolution to initialize positions and velocities. Set pixel format to `32-bit float (RGBA)` for precision. The noise provides random starting positions and velocities for each particle.

sections.title: Create the Simulation Feedback Loop
sections.content:
Create two **Feedback TOPs** — one for position and one for velocity. These capture the previous frame's state so the GLSL shader can read last frame's positions/velocities and compute the next frame.

Create a **GLSL TOP** with two inputs: the fed-back position texture and the fed-back velocity texture. The GLSL TOP will output updated positions (and optionally updated velocities on a second output).
sections.code:
# Set up the GLSL TOP for particle simulation
glsl_sim = op('/project1/glsl_sim')
glsl_sim.par.resolutionw = 256
glsl_sim.par.resolutionh = 256
glsl_sim.par.pixelformat = '32-bit float (RGBA)'
glsl_sim.par.outputresolution = 'custom'

sections.title: Write the Simulation Shader
sections.content:
The pixel shader reads the previous position and velocity for each particle (pixel), applies forces like gravity and damping, then outputs the new position. Each pixel's UV coordinate maps to a unique particle.

The shader runs once per particle per frame, making it massively parallel on the GPU.

sections.title: Render Particles with Instancing
sections.content:
To render the particles, create a **Geometry COMP** with a small sphere or point sprite inside. Enable **Instancing** on the Geometry COMP and use a **TOP to CHOP** to convert the position texture into instance transform channels.

Alternatively, use a **GLSL MAT** as the material and sample the position texture directly in the vertex shader using `gl_InstanceID` to look up each particle's position.
sections.code:
# Configure instancing on the Geometry COMP
geo = op('/project1/geo1')
geo.par.instancing = True
geo.par.instancetx = 'tx'
geo.par.instancety = 'ty'
geo.par.instancetz = 'tz'

# The CHOP feeding instance channels comes from
# converting the position texture via topTo CHOP

sections.title: Add Camera and Render
sections.content:
Create a **Camera COMP** and a **Render TOP** to view your particle system. Point the camera at the origin where particles spawn. The Render TOP composites everything in the 3D scene.

Adjust the GLSL uniforms (`uGravity`, `uDamping`) to control particle behavior. Try adding wind forces, attractors, or color based on velocity for more interesting effects.
sections.code:
# Set up the render pipeline
cam = op('/project1/cam1')
cam.par.tz = 5  # Pull camera back to see particles

render = op('/project1/render1')
render.par.camera = cam.path
